author: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy
book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

In this book Augustin refutes the second letter which Petilianus wrote to him after having seen the first of Augustin’s earlier books. This letter had been full of violent language; and Augustin rather shows that the arguments of Petilianus had been deficient and irrelevant, than brings forward arguments in support of his own statements.

title: Chapter 11

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: What wonder is it then, if, when I draw in the grain that has been shaken forth from the threshing-floor of the Lord, together with the soil and chaff, I suffer injury from the dust that rebounds against me; or that, when I am diligently seeking after the lost sheep of my Lord, I am torn by the briars of thorny tongues?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: I entreat you, lay aside for a time all considerations of party feeling, and judge with some degree of fairness between Petilianus and myself.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: I am desirous that you should be acquainted with the cause of the Church; he, that you should be familiar with mine.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For what other reason than because he dares not bid you disbelieve my witnesses, whom I am constantly citing in the cause of the Church,—for they are prophets and apostles, and Christ Himself, the Lord of prophets and apostles,—whereas you easily give him credit in whatever he may choose to say concerning me, a man against a man, and one, moreover, of your own party against a stranger to you?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And should I adduce any witnesses to my life, however important the thing he might say would be, it would not be believed by them, and of this Petilianus would quickly persuade you; especially when any one would bring forward a plea for me.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Since he is an enemy of the Donatist party, in virtue of this fact he would also continually be considered your enemy.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Whenever he aims any abuse at me, of whatever character it may be, you all applaud and shout assent.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: This cause he has found wherein the victory is possible for him, but only with you for judges.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: He will seek for neither proof nor witness; for all that he has to prove in his words is this, that he lavishes most copious abuse on one whom you most cordially hate.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For whereas, when the testimony of divine Scripture is quoted in such abundance and in such express terms in favor of the Catholic Church, he remains silent amidst your grief, he has chosen for himself a subject on which he may speak amidst applause from you; and though really conquered, yet, pretending that he stands unmoved, he may make statements concerning me like this, and even worse than this.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: It is enough for me, in respect of the cause which I am now pleading, that whatsoever I may be found to be, yet the Church for which I speak unconquered.
